headline: BEANS AND WHEAT CLOSE LOUD; CORN SPLITS IN TWO
subheadline: Old-crop corn fell hard Friday while new-crop held. Beans, wheat, hogs, and crude all finished higher. The week ahead brings pollination weather and a complex that is anything but quiet.
lead: Friday's close handed you a market that can't agree with itself. Old-crop corn fell 16½ cents while December added 6¼ cents, soybeans closed at $12.04 on 340,000 MT of fresh Chinese sales confirmed that morning, and wheat pushed to $6.83. Meanwhile, hogs ran 16.9% on the week, crude pushed past $82 again, and Class III milk dropped 11.1%. A StoneX economist said it plainly this week: demand and geopolitics are running these markets, not the heat dome. The Corn Belt's weather has been manageable through pollination so far, but the calendar and the crop condition reports next week will tell you whether that premium stays dormant or finally wakes up.
the_takeaway: Beans and wheat want higher; old-crop corn broke. Let the Monday crop report settle the question.
teaser: Old-crop corn split from new-crop, beans got Chinese money, and hogs had their best week in months. Here is what it all means heading into a critical pollination week.

one_number.unit: reciprocal tariff on Brazilian ethanol imports
one_number.context: The U.S. Trade Representative formalized 25% retaliatory tariffs on Brazilian goods, including ethanol, following a Section 301 investigation. That is structurally bullish for U.S. corn ethanol demand: Brazilian product gets priced out of the U.S. market at the margin. Watch how the corn crush spread responds early in the week, because this tariff did not get priced into Friday's close in any obvious way.

sections.title: Corn Splits at the Seam
sections.body: Old-crop corn dropped 16½ cents Friday, falling 3.6% to $4.45, while December held and actually added 6¼ cents to $4.67. That spread, now **22 cents wide**, is the market's clearest editorial: old crop is being abandoned, new crop is being bought. The catalyst was not weather. StoneX's Arlan Suderman told Brownfield the heat dome has not been a serious Corn Belt threat, and with China following through on demand commitments, the trade is rotating attention forward. The front month is under pressure from basis softness and a lack of new export demand. Next Monday's crop progress report at 3 PM CT is the first data point that could change the weather-premium calculus. If the good-to-excellent rating drops more than three points, that December contract runs. If the rating holds, old crop stays capped and the split stays intact.
sections.catalyst: StoneX economist: demand and geopolitics driving grains, not heat dome.
sections.bottom_line: Old crop broken, new crop held. Monday crop progress sets the next line.
sections.farmer_action: Old-crop corn still unpriced in the bin: basis improvement is your only remaining upside in the front month. If local basis is not firming this week, move it.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: old/new split confirmed and widened exactly as the carry structure telegraphed.

sections.title: Beans Get the Chinese Bid
sections.body: Soybeans closed at $12.04 nearby and $12.03 November, both up roughly a dollar and change on the week and at 93-94% of their 52-week range. The fuel on Friday was specific: USDA announced pre-open flash sales of 340,000 MT to China, 256,634 MT to Mexico, and 120,000 MT to unknown destinations before the bell. China's $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment through 2028, announced in May, is now showing up in repeated flash-sale execution rather than just diplomatic language. **Soybean oil also ran hard, gaining 5.0% to $74.81**, while meal gave back a dime to $320.20, meaning the market is pricing vegetable oil demand, not protein demand, as the marginal driver. The November contract at $12.03 is sitting at 94% of its 52-week range. Resistance is not far. A move through $12.20 next week would confirm the funds are adding, not just covering.
sections.catalyst: USDA pre-market flash sales: 340,000 MT soybeans to China plus 256,634 MT to Mexico, July 18.
sections.bottom_line: China is buying; oil share running; $12.20 is the line that opens the next leg.
sections.farmer_action: New-crop beans at $12.03: if you have less than 50% of expected production priced, this is a window worth using on 10-15% more before pollination weather enters the story.

sections.title: Wheat Closes at a 52-Week High Neighborhood
sections.body: Wheat finished at $6.83, up 12 cents Friday and sitting at 92% of its 52-week range. This is not a weather story domestically. The Hormuz premium in energy markets, the broader geopolitical bid across commodities, and reciprocal tariff noise globally are giving wheat a floor that its own supply fundamentals might not justify alone. The 52-week positioning tells you the funds are not fighting this market. **At $6.83, the question next week is whether wheat can hold this level without a new geopolitical catalyst**, because the domestic crop is not in distress. Winter wheat harvest is underway in the southern Belt; pressure from harvest-season cash sales will be real. Watch Thursday's export sales at 7:30 AM CT for confirmation that foreign demand is absorbing what harvest is delivering.
sections.catalyst: Geopolitical and demand-driven grain bid; Hormuz-premium energy complex supporting commodity complex broadly.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat firm but needs export confirmation to hold $6.83 through harvest pressure.

sections.title: Hogs Run; Cattle Hold Their Ground
sections.body: Lean hogs closed at $101.65, a 16.9% weekly gain, the biggest run in this contract in months. Live cattle added 0.6% to $224.43 but feeders gave back 0.3% to $345.95, and the cash market was the story: a sharp drop in direct cash prices pushed most futures months lower mid-week before buyers came back into live cattle by Friday. **The Cargill Fort Morgan lockout, ongoing since May 19, continues to create processing-constrained dynamics rather than supply-driven ones**. With roughly 2% of weekly slaughter capacity still disrupted, packer leverage remains elevated, and cash prices are not following the futures board cleanly. Hog momentum at $101.65 is a different animal: look at box-beef cutout data early next week to see whether the spread between live cattle value and hog value is signaling a protein substitution trade or purely technical fund positioning in hogs.
sections.catalyst: Cargill processing disruption suppressing live cattle cash prices; hogs ran on technical and fund buying.
sections.bottom_line: Hogs ran loud; cattle stuck in a cash-market squeeze that doesn't resolve without the lockout ending.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: cash-market squeeze held as the ceiling. Hogs confirmed the weekly run.

sections.title: Crude Holds $82; Energy Firms Into the Week
sections.body: WTI crude closed at $82.49, up 4.2% Friday and sitting at 43% of its 52-week range. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, created a volatile backdrop all week, but the EIA's Cushing data showing inventories that dipped below 20 million barrels earlier in July is providing a physical floor. Baker Hughes reported the U.S. rig count reached 588, up 44 from a year ago, which says production is responding, but not fast enough to move the price meaningfully lower on its own. **Natural gas added 1.7% to $2.91**, still in the basement at 8% of its 52-week range, but the European heatwave reported over the weekend adds a demand wildcard for LNG exports. Harvest-season diesel unhedged beyond this week faces an energy complex where the Hormuz premium is deflating slowly, not breaking down.
sections.catalyst: Cushing inventories below 20 million barrels through early July; Hormuz premium persisting despite diplomatic progress.
sections.bottom_line: Crude has a physical floor at Cushing; lock remaining harvest diesel needs before the next Hormuz headline.
sections.farmer_action: Any harvest-season diesel still unhedged: $82.49 with a Cushing floor and Hormuz premium still in the price is not the moment to wait for a pullback. Lock it.

outside_the_pit.title: 25% U.S. Tariff on Brazilian Ethanol Reshapes Corn Demand Math
outside_the_pit.body: The U.S. Trade Representative's 25% retaliatory tariff on Brazilian goods, including ethanol, structurally improves U.S. corn ethanol's domestic market position. Brazilian ethanol had been gaining share; that channel is now priced out at the margin. Corn crush demand benefiting from this shift was not in Friday's front-month close.

outside_the_pit.title: Foreign-Owned Farmland Faces Growing Federal and State Scrutiny
outside_the_pit.body: USDA and multiple state legislatures are tightening review of foreign farmland ownership, with particular attention to adversarial-nation buyers near military installations. For land-market participants, expect regulatory friction to become a pricing factor in certain geographies over the next 12-18 months.

outside_the_pit.title: Europe's Heatwave Tightening LNG Export Demand from U.S. Gulf
outside_the_pit.body: An intense early-summer heatwave in Europe is straining power generation and driving natural gas demand at a moment when U.S. LNG export capacity is near full utilization. If the heat persists through late July, U.S. natural gas at $2.91 is not reflecting the export pull that is building.

the_more_you_know.title: The soybean oil/meal split is telling you where China's money actually went.
the_more_you_know.body: Soybeans closed at $12.04 Friday on 340,000 MT of confirmed Chinese purchases, but look at where the value moved inside the crush: soybean oil ran 5.0% to $74.81 while soybean meal barely moved, down just a dime. That ratio shift matters. When China buys beans for protein, meal leads and oil follows. When China and global biodiesel demand are bidding on vegetable oil, oil leads and meal lags. Friday's price action inside the crush is saying this is an energy and renewable fuel trade, not a feed trade. The Brazilian 25% ethanol tariff announced this week reinforces the same theme: renewable fuel policy is redirecting vegetable oil demand toward U.S. soy. If you are pricing new-crop beans, you are not just pricing a protein commodity right now. You are pricing a fuel feedstock, and that changes the ceiling.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn good-to-excellent rating is the key number. If it drops three or more points from last week, a weather premium finally enters December corn. If it holds or improves, demand is still the only driver.

Weekly Export Sales: soybeans under 300,000 MT total would say Friday's flash-sale momentum isn't carrying into the new week. Above 400,000 MT with China as the primary buyer confirms the demand story is real.
Cargill Fort Morgan lockout: any resolution announcement would immediately reprice live cattle and feeder spreads. No resolution expected, but it is the binary event the cattle market is waiting on.

Hormuz diplomatic developments: the Strait of Hormuz premium has been deflating on U.S.-Iran back-channel talks via Swiss intermediaries since early April. A concrete diplomatic development would take crude below $80 fast. No deal yet; watch for any State Department statement.

Corn December $4.65: the line that matters. If crop progress disappoints Monday and December holds above $4.65, the carry trade is working and the demand/weather combo could push toward $4.80. If crop progress is clean and December fades below $4.65, old crop is taking new crop with it.

daily_quote.text: A patient man will eat ripe fruit.

source_summary: CME Group Friday settlement prices; USDA flash export sales July 18, 2026; Brownfield Ag News; StoneX/Arlan Suderman via Brownfield; Baker Hughes rig count; EIA Cushing inventory data; OilPrice.com; Feedstuffs; AgWeb; TheFencePost.
